Generated by ChatGPT

5 min read
Just now

--

“Big Tech companies have invested billions of dollars in creating closed AI models. But could it eventually turn out that free (open-source) versions created by anyone will be the true victor? Here are some reasons why open-source will dominate the future.”
At the beginning of 2024, Meta released Llama 3, not only with the open weights but also as a model capable of competing with the best closed systems. By 2026, things have changed dramatically; Llama 4, Qwen 2.5, DeepSeek R1 and Mistral Large 2 all now meet or exceed the performance of GPT 4O and Claude 3.5 on important benchmarks, and all of these models are free to download, modify, and deploy.
The first wave of AI was developed by big technology companies; the second wave is being created in public view by an international community.
What Open-Source AI Actually Means Today
When talking about ‘open-source AI’, the scope goes far beyond the fact that it can be ‘used for free or at no cost’, in addition there are considered to be three different items associated with a specific piece of AI:
1. Open Weights — These refer to weights associated with the trained model(s), which are released as open-source/publicly available; typically this is the most used and accessed example of ‘open’.
2. Open Code — These refer to all of the tools associated with accessing, utilizing these models, as well as, information regarding how to build/train these models using other ‘open-source’ tools.
3. Open Data — These are relatively few examples of open-source AI, generally they are either difficult to find or do not exist.
Conversely, there are many closed-source models like GPT-5 or Gemini 2.0 that have a fee structure based on usage i.e. pay per token via API.
The leading candidates for 2026 include:
- Meta’s Llama Series
- Mistral from France
- Alibaba Qwen xAI Grok-1 (partially open) DeepSeek (China)
Hugging Face (open-source community) will continue to act as the ‘social/community networking hub’ for open-source AI.
The Explosive Growth: From Underdog to Contender
Two years ago, it was funny to think of competing against Open Models. It’s not so funny now.
Llama 3 (in 2024) proved that an Open Model could reach the level of frontier models. By the end of 2025, those differences had been little to nothing.
As of 2026, numerous open source models dominate public leaderboards for reasoning, coding, and multilingual tasks regularly.
The community effect is second-to-none; whenever Meta publishes a new version of Llama, many different fine-tuned instances will become available shortly thereafter — tailored to various domains such as healthcare, legal services, specific local languages (like Urdu or Punjabi), and creative writing , etc.
Why Open-Source Has a Massive Edge
1. Blazing Speed of Innovation:
Open-source allows you to implement a new process discovered on a Friday across numerous different models by Friday; in other words, you can rapidly deploy new processes into production.
2. Radical Cost Advantage:
A powerful 70B model can be run locally or on inexpensive cloud-based G-PS for pocket change rather than pay for API hits, so this could be a huge cost reduction for startups, freelancers, and businesses throughout the world, especially in countries such as Pakistan, due to there not being any unexpected charges when their AI usage goes up.
3. True Customization & Privacy:
Do you want to use an AI that does not send anything back to the USA? An open source model can be fine-tuned and run on your hardware, allowing you to do this easily.
Providers (healthcare, banks) and Government agencies are all interested in using this because they can have complete control over the ethical and bias filters, and they can also control/how their AI(s) will behave.
4. Global Democratization:
There is not just a limited number of talent coming out of the Silicon Valley area; thanks to open-source code, the restrictions of geographic boundaries no longer exist.
There are now developers from Gujranwala, Lahore, Bangalore and Sâo Paulo who have the ability to create, fine-tune and/or lead the next big creation in software technology. Open-source is the key to this abundance of talent.
5. No Vendor Lock-In:
Are you tired of OpenAI’s frequent changes to pricing or policies? Hurry up and switch to an open source model without interruption to your operations.
Real-World Proof It’s Already Happening
Open models are the backbone of many new startups, including Perplexity AI. They are easy on the budget and fast.
A lot of enterprises are moving work that contains sensitive data to an open-source platform without any of the distractions that go with traditional business activities.
There is a major increase in local AI applications (apps that run fully on your mobile device or laptop) due to the availability of quantized open models.
Some open model code benchmarks now surpass the coding performance of earlier GPT-4 versions.
My Prediction: The Hybrid Future:
I anticipate that by 2028–2030, more than 80% (or 80%+) of the use cases for AI will exist in open-source. This means that all but the most bleeding-edge frontier models that require enormous amounts of computing resources (costing billions) can be found in the open sourced world.
While Big Tech will probably continue to release potent base (foundation) models, the real value and creativity will come from the open-source ecosystem created to improve, customize, and implement these foundation models or base models in the long run.
Thus, the locus of power in AI will shift from those with the greatest quantity of GPUs to the strongest, most collaborative communities, thus democratizing AI.
What This Means for You:
The future of AI will not take place in the corporate world. You can download it now.
Here are some ways you can get started:
Use either Ollama or LM Studio to locally run the Llama 3.1 or Qwen2.5 models on your computer.
Visit the Hugging Face website and search for thousands of model options (types).
Connect with other people around the world through Discord servers and Reddit r/LocalLLaMA.
Open-source AI helps eliminate barriers for anyone who is a writer, developer, student, or business owner in Pakistan (the rest of the world too).
This movement has already begun, you can be apart of it too, as long as you have internet access.
Final thoughts:
What do you believe? Will the open-source “community” have enough momentum to enact change in the technology world? Or will the big companies have an unassailable advantage over the community? Leave a comment and let me know if you agree or disagree, I read all of the comments.
If this information helped answer your question, give it a thumbs up and follow me as I continue posting articles on AI through the end of the year.
